content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
April 29,
1992
Fred S.
Etheridge
Meyers,
Nave, Riback
& West
Gateway Plaza
777 Davis Street,
Suite 300
San Leandro,
CA
94577
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A—92-157
Dear Mr.
Etheridge:
This letter is in response to your request for advice under
the conflict of interest code provisions of the Political Reform
Act. 1/
QUESTION
Must the conflict of interest code for the Fire Districts
Association of California designate the full 95—member board or
will designation of the board’s executive committee suffice?
CONCLUSION
The conflict of interest code for the Fire Districts
Association of California must designate the full 95-member board.
FACTS
Your law firm represents the Fire Districts Association of
California
-- Fire Association Self Insurance System
(FDAC).
The
FDAC is a joint powers agency comprised of fire districts.
The
district is in the process of adopting a conflict of interest code
and a question has arisen regarding the designation of its
95—member board.
1/
Government Code Sections 81000—91015.
All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000,
q.
All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


The FDAC was created to manage its members’ workers
compensation matters and currently has 95 member districts from
throughout Northern and Southern California.
Each member district
elects one representative to the FDAC Board of Directors.
Because
of the size of the 95-member board,
and the fact that its member
districts are located throughout the state, the FDAC has
established a seven—member executive committee to be responsible
for the operation of the FDAC.
The 95—member board meets once a year to elect directors to
the seven—member executive committee,
adopt the annual budget for
the FDAC, vote on admitting new member districts,
and adopt an
annual audit.
A typical meeting is completed in less than an
hour.
The seven—member executive committee meets three to four
times a year and is the body which makes decisions on contracts,
investments,
claim processing and the work of consultants.
Essentially, it is the executive dommittee which runs the
operations of the FDAC.
ANALYSIS
V
Government Code Section 87302 requires that a conflict of
interest code specifically enumerate the positions within an
agency which involve the making or participating in the making of
decisions which may foreseeably have a material effect on any
financial interest,
and for each such enumerated position, the
specific types of investments, business positions, interests in
real property,
and sources of income which shall be made
reportable.
A public official
2
/
“makes a governmental
decision” when he or she, acting within the
authority of his or her office:
(1)
Votes on a matter;
(2)
Appoints a person;
(3)
Obligates or commits his or her agency to
any course of action;
(4)
Enters into any contractual agreement on
behalf of his or her agency;
2/
“Public official” means every member, officer,
employee or
V
consultant of a state or local government agency.
V (Section
82048)


(5)
Determines not to act, within the meaning
of subparagraphs
(1),
(2),
(3)
and (4),
unless such determination is made because
of his or her financial interest.
Regulation 18700 (b)
A public official “participates in the making
of a governmental decision” when, acting within the
authority of his or her position,
he or she:
(1)
Negotiates, without significant
substantive review, with a governmental
entity or private person regarding the
decision; or
(2)
Advises or makes recommendations to the
decision—maker, either directly or
without significant intervening
substantive review,
by:
(A)
Conducting research or making any
investigation which requires the
exercise of judgment on the part of
the official and the purpose of
which is to influence the decision;
or
(B)
Preparing or presenting any report,
analysis or opinion, orally or in
writing, which requires the exercise
of judgment on the part of the
official and the purpose of which is
to influence the decision.
Regulation 18700(c)
Even though the board meets only once a year and has a very
limited decision-making role, it is voting on approval of the
budget and electing persons to the board.
Accordingly, members of
the board participate in the making of governmental decisions and
are subject to the provisions of the Act.
Since the board makes and participates in the making of
decisions, the conflict of interest code for the Fire Districts
Association of California must include the members of the board as
designated positions.
To assist you in the code development process,
I have
enclosed the procedural regulation, additional fact sheets,
as
well as a sample of a conflict of interest code for another self
insured joint powers agency.


If I can assist you further in the code adoption process,
please do not hesitate to call me at
(916)
322—5901.
Sincerely,
Jeanette E. Turvill
Political Reform Consultant
Legal Division
JET/jt
Enclosures

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Must the conflict of interest code for the Fire Districts
Association of California designate the full 95—member board or
will designation of the board’s executive committee suffice?

CONCLUSION: The conflict of interest code for the Fire Districts
Association of California must designate the full 95-member board.
